summary: American Pop artist behind the LOVE sculptures (tilted O, usually red letters). Changed surname to his native state. Also created HOPE, EAT sculpture, Hebrew AHAVA version for Israel Museum.

works.name: LOVE (1964–)
works.description: Stacked letters L-O-V-E with tilted O. First used in 1965 MoMA Christmas card. Red letters are most common. Hebrew version AHAVA for Israel Museum. Park in Philadelphia nicknamed for the sculpture. General Idea parodied it for AIDS awareness. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Stacked four letters with tilted O; first used in 1965 MoMA Christmas card; park in Philadelphia nicknamed for it; Hebrew version (AHAVA) for Israel Museum
examples: This artist first used his most-famous motif in a 1965 Christmas card he designed for the Museum of Modern Art
A park in Philadelphia is nicknamed for a sculpture by this artist whose second letter is a tilted 'O'

clues.clue: General Idea (Toronto collective) parodied it for AIDS awareness billboards
examples: The Toronto-based artist collective General Idea parodied this artist's most iconic work to raise awareness for AIDS

clues.clue: Changed surname to the name of his native state
examples: who changed his surname to the name of his native state

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Pop artist. Born Robert Clark. EAT sculpture at 1964 NY World's Fair confused visitors. In Warhol's film Kiss, kissing Marisol Escobar. Numbers 1-0 painted aluminum sculptures. Elegies for Marsden Hartley with dates 1914 and 1989. Maps of southern states surrounded by rings.

clues.clue: EAT lighted sculpture at 1964 NY World's Fair confused visitors who thought it was a restaurant
examples: Visitors at the 1964 New York World's Fair were confused by a lighted sculpture by this pop artist of the word 'EAT'

clues.clue: In Andy Warhol's film Kiss, kissing Marisol Escobar
examples: In Andy Warhol's film Kiss, this pop artist can be seen kissing Marisol Escobar

clues.clue: Elegies for Marsden Hartley with dates 1914 and 1989
examples: This artist included the dates 1914 and 1989 in the first of a series of elegies for Marsden Hartley

comprehensive_summary:
Robert Indiana (born Robert Clark) was an American Pop artist who changed his surname to that of his native state. He is best known for the LOVE sculpture, featuring stacked letters with a tilted O. He first used the motif in a 1965 Christmas card for MoMA and created a Hebrew version (AHAVA) for the Israel Museum. A park in Philadelphia is nicknamed for the sculpture. The Toronto collective General Idea parodied it for AIDS awareness.

Other works include EAT (a lighted sculpture at the 1964 NY World's Fair that confused visitors), Numbers 1-0 (painted aluminum), and elegies for Marsden Hartley. He appeared in Andy Warhol's film Kiss, kissing Marisol Escobar.
